summary: Hotz documents his attempt to replace his MacBook with a Linux laptop, specifically an HP ZBook with AMD's Strix Halo chip. While he's made progress on sleep power management (0.2W lid-closed), active use still draws 7-10W compared to MacBook's efficiency, giving only 7 hours of battery life. He identifies the CPU's chiplet design as the main culprit, drawing 4W at idle versus Apple's 1W. He calls on AMD and HP to release better documentation and argues that with proper optimization, a competitive Linux laptop is achievable.
key_points: Successfully switched from iPhone to Samsung Z Fold, but laptops are harder because no hardware matches MacBook quality
Asahi Linux isn't viable long-term due to Apple's custom MCUs and missing features like external USB-C monitor support
AMD Strix Halo's chiplet architecture is power-hungry, drawing 4W at idle versus Apple M3 Max's 1W
LLMs are making reverse engineering fun again by helping decode embedded controller bytes and power management
Hardware vendors should release schematics or at least block diagrams like Framework does
money_quotes: Sadly, there's no better laptop hardware than a MacBook.
It fixed the problem I always had with Arch, which is how customizable it is.
LLMs are super helpful at reverse engineering – it's almost becoming fun again.
That's only 7 hours of life, which is 30% of a MacBook, and still not really acceptable.
You know MoP is a good idea because Intel is discontinuing it while Apple keeps doing it.
Don't turn on the stupid keyboard backlight that draws 2 watts!
I bet there's something stupid or poorly designed, HP should release a schematic of the laptop.
key_insight: Despite AMD's Strix Halo being the only chip rivaling Apple silicon in performance, its chiplet design and lack of vendor transparency on power management make Linux laptops still far behind MacBooks in battery efficiency.
